We've already considered the damaging long-term effects to an individual of engaging in unethical attempts to succeed. Recently, my colleagues and I conducted research to demonstrate that this damage isn't restricted to individual enactors, but also applies to organizations that use unethical practices to succeed. Studies have demonstrated that over half of business leaders admit to taking unethical actions to reach their financial goals. Because of the powerful draw of perceived financial benefits, it was clear to us that persuading leaders to employ ethical, that is, sleuth-like influence approaches, and to avoid unethical, smuggler-like ones, it would require more than shouting ethics at them. It would require making the case that the systematic use of ethically objectionable influence tactics ultimately becomes a financially self-damaging process. We thought that one effective way to make the case may be to track the hidden costs to an organization that trains, or expects, or simply allows its influence agents—that is, marketers, salespeople, copywriters, recruiters, negotiators—to employ deceptive practices on individuals who are outside the organization, such as customers or clients. I think it is appropriate to label these injurious costs as tumors because they have a malignant nature growing, spreading, and eating progressively at the organization's health and vigor, often below the surface. We thought we could identify three major such losses that together form the triple tumor structure of organizational dishonesty. They involve three features of a commercial organization known to ravage its health—poor employee performance, high employee turnover, and employee fraud and malfeasance. The price tag of each can be staggering.
